Contenido:
Prehistory is the longest period of human history, spanning from the appearance of the first hominids to the invention of writing. It is traditionally divided into the Paleolithic, Neolithic, and Metal Ages.

The Paleolithic extends from about 2.5 million years ago to 10,000 BC. It is the period of hunter-gatherers who took advantage of all the resources offered by the natural environment. This period is subdivided into:

Lower Paleolithic: Period in which the first stone tools appear, pebbles worked with a single edge.

Middle Paleolithic: Development of knapping techniques, such as Levallois and discoid knapping, which allowed for more regular flakes for making different tools. Notable among these is the hand axe, a lithic tool knapped on both sides used for cutting, scraping, and digging.

Upper Paleolithic: During this time, blade knapping technique developed, allowing for better use of flint, as well as the creation of more varied and specialized tools, including arrowheads, scrapers, burins, and cutting blades.

Materials and Tools: The material used in the Paleolithic was stone, mainly quartzite and limestone or flint, worked using percussion technique. One stone was struck against another to obtain fragments or flakes, which could also be further worked to create a wide variety of tools. From the Upper Paleolithic onwards, bone and antler also began to be used to make harpoons, needles, and points or spears, demonstrating greater technical sophistication. All these tools were essential for survival, hunting, food processing, and clothing making.

Art and Beliefs: During the Upper Paleolithic, ritual burials with grave goods became consolidated and developed. Furthermore, artistic manifestations, which reach their highest expression in cave art, became a new form of expression showing the high degree of complexity achieved by our species.

Título: The Neolithic. The First Farmers and Herders
Contenido:
Between 10,000 BC and 4,500 BC, the first sedentary communities whose economy was based on agriculture and livestock began to develop in the Near East. This stage represents one of the most transcendental changes in human history, favoring the growth of human groups and their social complexity, as well as the appearance of new beliefs. This period is characterized by the emergence of important technological innovations such as the production of pottery and polished stone objects, as well as the development of new types of tools.

Technology and Materials: If the Paleolithic was characterized by knapped stone, the Neolithic is characterized by polished stone. The polishing technique consists of polishing or rubbing the surface of stones (such as diorite, basalt, or jade) against other abrasive stones until a smooth surface is achieved. This technique, slower than percussion, allowed for more resistant and effective edges for new agricultural tasks, such as hoes and, especially, polished axes for felling forests and preparing crop fields.

Another of the most notable contributions of this period was the appearance of pottery. Ceramic containers allowed for the storage of surplus grain and liquids, as well as the processing, cooking, and consumption of food. Soon, these clay objects, molded and fired, had an artistic as well as utilitarian character.

Cardial Pottery: One of the most distinctive forms of early Neolithic pottery in the Mediterranean is Cardial (or impressed) pottery. Its name comes from the use of the toothed edge of a marine shell, Cardium edule (or cockle), to make decorative impressions on the soft clay before firing. These geometric designs, often in horizontal bands, are a key cultural marker of the expansion of the first Neolithic populations along the coasts of southern Europe. Other distinctive decorations of this period included relief decoration, incised decoration made using punches, impressed decoration with bone combs, and decoration with ochre paint.

The Bronze Age is a period of Prehistory, within the Metal Ages, that developed approximately between 3000 and 800 BC. Its defining feature is the discovery and widespread use of the alloy of copper and tin to create bronze. This material is harder, more malleable, and more resistant than pure copper, allowing for the mass production of weapons and tools, as well as ensuring subsequent recycling of the material. The greater social complexity of this period can be seen in the size and organization of settlements, the sophistication of funerary rituals, and the presence of ornamental items made from exotic materials. These types of objects demonstrate the exchange of prestige goods among the incipient social elites of these communities.

Smelting: The main technical innovation was smelting. The process first requires the extraction of minerals (copper and tin cassiterite) and their smelting in furnaces at high temperatures. Subsequently, the molten material is poured into stone molds that shape various objects: rectangular-section axes, chisels, arrowheads, riveted daggers, metal awls, and personal adornments such as bracelets.

Characteristic ceramic forms of the Valencian Bronze: The 'Valencian Bronze' has its own characteristics that distinguish it from neighboring cultures like 'El Argar'. These differences are also manifested in the forms of its ceramic material. Valencian Bronze pottery is characterized by being handmade and generally undecorated. The most common ceramic forms are large jars, cheese pots, casseroles, cooking pots, bowls, and twin vessels with basic functions of storage, processing, cooking, and food consumption. In later phases, carinated vessels appear, featuring a profile characterized by a very pronounced change in angle. Other materials such as bone and ivory were used to make characteristic V-perforated buttons. All these materials are testimony to an economy based on agriculture and livestock, complemented by a developing metallurgy.

Between the 6th and 1st centuries BC, the Mediterranean coast of the Iberian Peninsula was home to a group of peoples that classical authors called 'Iberians'. Despite sharing common traits such as their language, they never constituted a true political unity and occasionally engaged in conflicts among themselves and against other neighboring peoples. The dominant classes exercised political and economic control from the cities. Around them, rural settlements (farmsteads and labor houses) dedicated to the exploitation of agricultural and livestock resources, which were the bases of their economy, were established. Forts and watchtowers intended for border surveillance and defense were placed in strategic points of the territory. The family and its clientele networks were the foundation of a clearly hierarchical society.

Pottery: The potter's wheel and the two-chamber kiln allowed for the production of ceramics with very fine walls and homogeneous finishes. The oldest decorative motifs are geometric, and over time vegetal, animal, and human elements were added, eventually distinguishing two great artistic circles: the narrative style of Liria and the symbolic style of Elche-Archena. Their wide variety of forms reflects a great diversity of uses (pantry, transport, cooking, ritual, etc.).

At sites like Tossal de Sant Miquel (Llíria), vessels with rich figurative decoration have been found, depicting everyday, ritual, or warlike scenes, such as warrior parades, dances, musicians, or mythical animals. Their informative and artistic value makes them a fundamental testimony of Iberian culture. In parallel, luxury tableware from the central Mediterranean was imported and sometimes imitated. This pottery, known as black glaze or Campanian ware, is distinguished by its very refined paste and dark, almost metallic gloss (obtained through a special firing in a two-chamber kiln), imitating the forms of prestigious metal tableware.

The Iberians were the first peninsular peoples to mint coins on a large scale, at local mints such as Arse (Sagunto) or Kelin, using their own alphabet. The most popular and characteristic design on the reverses of bronze coins (such as asses and their fractions) is the horseman. This figure, typically facing right and in full gallop or with a spear, symbolizes the importance of the horse. This animal was a clear symbol of social status, and occasionally, some have been found buried alongside their owners' tombs. The most common obverses feature a male figure, usually bearded and always looking to the right.

The history of Rome spans more than a thousand years, from its humble foundation by the Tiber, to its expansion and control of the entire Mediterranean, leaving an indelible mark on law, language, and Western culture. The occupation of Iberia is marked by the Punic Wars (246-146 BC) between Rome and Carthage. Rome's final triumph led to the progressive incorporation of the Iberian Peninsula into its territories and marked the beginning of a process that completely transformed territorial, social, and economic organization. Ancient Iberian cities, such as Saguntum or Lucentum (Alicante), were integrated into the new province, and new colonies like Valentia (138 BC) were founded.

Pottery: Terra Sigillata
The most characteristic pottery of Roman tableware is Terra Sigillata, developed mainly from the 1st century BC. It is made with a fine, reddish clay paste that has a characteristic bright reddish slip providing a glossy and impermeable surface, simulating the appearance of metal. The term sigillata refers to the stamps and potter's marks that the pieces often bore, as well as the relief decoration achieved using molds. This technique allowed for the rapid and mass production of bowls, plates, and cups with figurative and geometric motifs. Although the initial sigillata was imported (Italian or Gaulish), important workshops developed in the Iberian Peninsula, giving rise to Terra Sigillata Hispánica (TSH). This pottery was mainly wheel-made and characterized by a slip that gave it a smooth and shiny surface, with a finish that increased its resistance and durability.

During the Roman period, significant technical advances were made, highlighting their mastery of civil engineering with key infrastructures such as Roman roads. This extensive road network played a key role in the expansion and maintenance of the Empire, facilitating both military mobility and trade and the Romanization of the provinces. Also notable was the construction of cities with orthogonal layouts, urban sanitation systems, baths, and great hydraulic works, such as the Peña Cortada Aqueduct (between Calles and Llíria), which guaranteed a regular water supply for irrigation or consumption. Trade was the backbone of the Roman economy, allowing many products from Hispania, such as metals, salt, textiles, cereals, wine, etc., to flow throughout the Mediterranean, enriching its cities and financing Rome's military and civil power. The steelyard was a bronze instrument used for weighing objects or products. Its invention and mass use, together with the minting of standardized coins like the bronze as and sestertius, greatly facilitated trade, taxation, and daily economic life in the province. Regional specialization, with different provinces focused on the production of specific goods, allowed the exchange of ideas, technologies, and customs among the various regions of the empire, and constituted a true engine of prosperity and expansion for Rome.

The conservation of cultural heritage, especially archaeological objects, is structured around two fundamental and complementary disciplines: preventive conservation and restoration. Understanding the actions of both is essential to guarantee the preservation of these assets for future generations.

The main objective of preventive conservation is not to intervene directly on the object, but to act on its environment to slow down its deterioration and prevent future damage. It is a global and forward-looking strategy that seeks to minimize risks before they occur.

In contrast, restoration involves a direct intervention on the object to stop an active degradation process, consolidate its structure, or reintegrate its parts in order to facilitate its reading and interpretation.

Within preventive conservation, environmental control is a cornerstone. Factors such as intense light are extremely harmful, as they can cause irreversible discoloration, fragility, and accelerated aging in organic materials such as wood, textiles, and pigments.

Equally critical is the control of relative humidity. A high level of humidity drastically accelerates metal corrosion and promotes the growth of fungi and mold, which can irreversibly damage a wide variety of materials.
The goal is not to create an extremely dry environment, but to maintain stability within optimal parameters for each type of material. Preventive conservation measures also extend to storage and packaging solutions.

The use of stable materials, such as acid-free cardboard, is a fundamental practice. Its purpose is to prevent acidic components present in conventional packaging from transferring to objects, accelerating chemical degradation reactions such as paper acidification or metal corrosion.

When restoration is necessary, one of the most important ethical principles is that of reversibility. This means that the materials and techniques applied (adhesives, consolidants, etc.) must be removable in the future without damaging the original object. This principle aims to ensure that current interventions do not compromise the possibilities for action of future specialists, who will have even more advanced techniques and knowledge.

In conclusion, preventive conservation and restoration form an inseparable pair. The former constitutes the basis of responsible management, creating a stable environment that reduces the need for direct interventions. The latter acts as a specialized tool when deterioration is already present, always applied with scientific rigor and respect for the materiality and history of the object. Together, they ensure that our archaeological heritage endures over time.
